skillName: Perimeter of an isosceles triangle or an isosceles trapezium
misconceptionSummary: Students don't understand what 'isosceles' means geometrically, so they can't identify the equal sides.

visuals.ruleText: 'Isosceles' means exactly two sides are equal — marked with matching tick marks on diagrams
visuals.subText: The equal sides are called legs; the unequal side is the base

steps.narration: Isosceles comes from Greek meaning 'equal legs'. On a diagram, tick marks on two sides show they are equal. One tick on each of those sides — the base has no tick because it is different.

lines.text: Isosceles triangle has a line of symmetry
The axis runs from apex to midpoint of base
The two halves mirror each other → legs equal

steps.narration: An isosceles triangle has one line of symmetry through the apex — the top vertex. This symmetry is what makes the two legs equal: each is the mirror image of the other.

lines.text: Isosceles trapezium: symmetric about vertical axis

steps.narration: An isosceles trapezium also has a vertical line of symmetry. The two parallel sides are different lengths — that is what makes it a trapezium. The two non-parallel legs are mirror images — that is what makes it isosceles.

visuals.expression: Isosceles = one line of symmetry → two equal sides
visuals.label: Tick marks show equal sides — use symmetry to identify them

steps.narration: Symmetry reveals the equal sides. Find the axis of symmetry, identify the mirror-image sides — those are the equal legs for the perimeter formula.

misconceptionStrip.text: Common mistake: not identifying which sides are equal before calculating. Look for tick marks or the axis of symmetry — those reveal the two equal legs.
misconceptionStrip.audioNarration: Isosceles means one line of symmetry and two equal sides. Find the axis of symmetry first, then use 2 × leg + remaining sides for the perimeter.
